  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The reported source and sink are both real, so this is not a B1 issue: request-derived path data from `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` (`request.getServletPath()` / `getPathInfo()`) and, in servlet-filter mode, from `FilterTools.getRelativePath(...)`, can reach `AbstractFileResolvingResource.isReadable()` where `getFile()` is used. But the path does not reach the file API unchecked. In both concrete handlers, `ClassPathResourceHandler.getResource` and `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource`, the request path is normalized by `UriPath.canonical(path)` before resolution, and the resolved resource is then constrained to the configured static-resource base by `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstInClassPath(...)` / `protectAgainstForExternal(...)`. `ClassPathResource` also rejects `../`, `WEB-INF`, `META-INF`, and URL-like paths after path cleaning. So the CodeQL result reflects real taint flow but misses the custom sanitization and boundary-check logic. This is therefore a false positive caused by overlooked sanitizers/guards, not by unreachable control flow or wrong source/sink selection.",
  "FP_Reason": [
    "D1"
  ],
  "evidence": "Reachable flow exists: `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource` builds `pathInContext` from request path and passes it to the concrete handler (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59`), and `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers` only calls `resource.isReadable()` after `staticResourceHandler.getResource(httpRequest)` returns a resource (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:85-93`). The guards are in the concrete handlers: `ExternalResourceHandler` canonicalizes the user path with `UriPath.canonical(path)` and then checks `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` before returning the resource (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:65-83`); `ClassPathResourceHandler` does the analogous check with `protectAgainstInClassPath(...)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:66-84`). `UriPath.canonical` explicitly removes `.` / `..` and returns `null` when the path goes above root (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:25-30,151-153`). `DirectoryTraversal` throws if the resolved path is outside the configured folder (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:12-23,30-33`). `ClassPathResource.isInvalidPath` also rejects remaining `../` after `StringUtils.cleanPath` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:94-107`). The project has regression tests showing traversal attempts are blocked: `StaticFilesTest.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionLocal` expects HTTP 400 for a traversal payload (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java:140-147`), and `StaticFilesTestExternal.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionExternal` confirms no external-file disclosure (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:119-127`). Git history also shows commit `26b57d0` (`Fix for #700 - Arbitrary File Read Vulnerability`) introduced these traversal checks, indicating the current code is post-fix.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "report": "Rule Name: Uncontrolled data used in path expression
Rule Description: Accessing paths influenced by users can allow an attacker to access unexpected resources.
Severity: error
Detailed Message: This path depends on a [["user-provided value"|"relative:///src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:50:31:50:54"]].
This path depends on a [["user-provided value"|"relative:///src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:51:28:51:48"]].
This path depends on a [["user-provided value"|"relative:///src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:54:27:54:50"]].
This path depends on a [["user-provided value"|"relative:///src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:55:24:55:44"]].

Location-file: /src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java
Location-start-line: 57
Location-start-col: 16
Location-end-line: 57
Location-end-col: 19",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The source is real user input: `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` builds `pathInContext` from `request.getServletPath()` and `request.getPathInfo()` (and `FilterTools.getRelativePath()` derives the same from `request.getRequestURI()` in servlet mode). That path is used on the normal static-file serving path when an application enables `staticFiles.externalLocation(...)`. The sink is also real: `ExternalResource` creates a `File` from the resolved path, and `file.exists()` is invoked on that user-influenced filesystem path. More importantly, the code's intended traversal defense is flawed, so this is not a harmless pre-check. `UriPath.canonical()` only canonicalizes `/`-separated traversal, but `ExternalResource` later normalizes backslashes through `StringUtils.cleanPath()`. An attacker can therefore send backslash-based traversal that survives `UriPath.canonical()` and is only resolved later. The subsequent containment check in `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal()` is not path-safe: it uses a plain string prefix test (`startsWith`) instead of proper path-segment comparison. As a result, a path outside the base folder but sharing the same prefix can be accepted and then served. This is reachable in normal control flow and matches CWE-022; it is not an A1/A2/B1 false positive.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Reachable data flow: request path -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` -> `new ExternalResource(addedPath)` -> `ExternalResource.exists()` / later `getInputStream()`. Relevant code: `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource()` canonicalizes the request path at line 66, joins it with `baseResource` at line 68, constructs `ExternalResource` at line 70, and if the resource passes checks returns it at lines 81-83. `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume()` then serves the file by calling `resource.getInputStream()` and copying it to the HTTP response (lines 91-102). The intended sanitizer is broken: `DirectoryTraversal.isPathWithinFolder()` returns `removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(path).startsWith(removeLeadingAndTrailingSlashesFrom(folder))`. Example real-world exploit: if the app configures `staticFiles.externalLocation(\"/var/www/public\")`, a request for `/%2e%2e%5cpublic_backup%5csecret.txt` is decoded to `/..\\public_backup\\secret.txt`. `UriPath.canonical()` does not collapse that because it does not treat `\\` as a separator. `StringUtils.cleanPath()` in `ExternalResource` converts it to `/var/www/public/../public_backup/secret.txt`, which normalizes to `/var/www/public_backup/secret.txt`. The containment check then wrongly accepts it because `var/www/public_backup/secret.txt`.startsWith(`var/www/public`) is true, even though the file is outside the configured base directory. The resource is then returned and streamed to the client. Even before that, the reported sink `file.exists()` already probes an attacker-chosen path outside the base directory.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
1:
{
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The main sources are correctly identified in `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)`, where attacker-controlled `request.getServletPath()` and `request.getPathInfo()` are combined and passed into resource resolution. The `FilterTools.getRequestURI()` source is likely an over-approximation for this specific sink path, because static-file serving uses the original `httpRequest` in `SparkFilter`, but that does not invalidate the finding since the `AbstractResourceHandler` sources are enough. The sink at `AbstractFileResolvingResource.isReadable()` is security-relevant because it resolves the attacker-influenced path to a `File`, and successful resolution is immediately followed by `resource.getInputStream()` and response streaming. The apparent sanitizers are incomplete: `UriPath.canonical()` only canonicalizes `/`-based `.` and `..` segments, while later `StringUtils.cleanPath()` converts `\\\\` to `/` and collapses `..`. After that, `DirectoryTraversal.isPathWithinFolder()` only checks `startsWith(...)` on strings, not a canonical path boundary. As a result, a request like `/%2e%2e%5cpublic2%5csecret.txt` can normalize from a base such as `/public` or `/var/www/public` to a sibling path like `/public2/secret.txt` or `/var/www/public2/secret.txt`, still pass the prefix check, and then be read and served. This is a real CWE-022 risk, not a shallow or unreachable flow.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Source flow: `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` reads `request.getServletPath()` / `request.getPathInfo()` and passes `addPaths(servletPath, pathInfo)` into `getResource(path)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/AbstractResourceHandler.java:40-59`). Reachable sink path: `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` calls `staticResourceHandler.getResource(httpRequest)`, then `resource.isReadable()`, then `resource.getInputStream()` and copies it to the HTTP response (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:91-103`). External handler: `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` runs `path = UriPath.canonical(path)`, builds `addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path)`, creates `new ExternalResource(addedPath)`, and only then checks `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:65-83`). `ExternalResource` uses `new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path))`, so backslashes are converted and `..` is collapsed (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResource.java:39-40`). The traversal guard is flawed because `DirectoryTraversal.isPathWithinFolder()` returns `rlatsPath.startsWith(rlatsFolder)` after trimming slashes (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:30-33`), so `/var/www/public2/secret.txt` is incorrectly treated as inside `/var/www/public`. `UriPath.canonical()` does not normalize backslashes (`src/main/java/spark/resource/UriPath.java:32-161`). The classpath handler has the same issue pattern (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResourceHandler.java:66-84`), and `ClassPathResource` only rejects paths that still contain `../` after cleaning (`src/main/java/spark/resource/ClassPathResource.java:94-110`). Runtime reachability is supported by the project's own tests, which send encoded backslash traversal payloads through this code path: `StaticFilesTest.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionLocal` and `StaticFilesTestExternal.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionExternal` (`src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java:141-145`, `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-125`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}",

Location-end-col: 39",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report follows a real call chain, so this is not an A1/A2 reachability issue: request path data from `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` can influence static-file lookup, and the sink `ExternalResource.getInputStream()` is a real file-read sink. However, this specific CWE-022 finding is still a false positive because the relevant path is sanitized and constrained before the sink is reachable. In `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:66`, the request path is normalized with `UriPath.canonical(path)`, which removes `.`/`..` segments and returns `null` for attempts to go above the root (see `src/test/java/spark/resource/UriPathTest.java`, e.g. `\"/../foo\" -> null` and `\"/foo/../../bar\" -> null`). After that, before any resource is returned, `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource)` is executed at `ExternalResourceHandler.java:82`; if the resolved file is outside the configured external static directory, an exception is thrown and the file is not served. `StaticFilesConfiguration.consume()` catches that condition and blocks the response instead of reaching `ExternalResource.java:67`. So the FP is mainly due to CodeQL missing the sanitizer/guard logic (D1). One reported source is also noisy: `FilterTools.getRelativePath()` is not the static-file source for this sink in `SparkFilter`, because static file consumption is invoked with the original `httpRequest`.",
  "FP_Reason": ["D1"],
  "evidence": "Key path: `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` calls `staticResourceHandler.getResource(httpRequest)` and only later calls `resource.getInputStream()` if a readable resource was returned (`src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesConfiguration.java:89-103`). In `src/main/java/spark/resource/ExternalResourceHandler.java:60-83`, the request-controlled path is processed as follows: `path = UriPath.canonical(path);` -> `final String addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path);` -> `ExternalResource resource = new ExternalResource(addedPath);` -> `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource);` before returning the resource. The guard in `src/main/java/spark/staticfiles/DirectoryTraversal.java:18-23` throws when the resolved path is outside the configured external folder. Tests show traversal handling exists in the codebase: `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTest.java:141-148` expects a traversal probe to be blocked, and `src/test/java/spark/staticfiles/StaticFilesTestExternal.java:120-128` verifies an encoded traversal-style request is not served. Also, `src/main/java/spark/servlet/SparkFilter.java:149-168` shows `FilterTools.getRelativePath()` is used for route matching, while static file serving is called with the original `httpRequest`, so that reported source is spurious for this sink.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "TP",
  "analysis": "The report is a true positive. The important source is the HTTP request path read in `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` from `request.getServletPath()` / `request.getPathInfo()`, which is passed into `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)`. The `FilterTools.getRequestURI()` source mentioned by CodeQL is not necessary for exploitability and may not be the exact runtime path for servlet static-file handling, but the `AbstractResourceHandler` sources are valid and sufficient. The sink is also real: the user-influenced path is used to build a `File` in `ExternalResource`, queried with `exists()` / `isDirectory()`, and if accepted is later opened and returned to the client. There are partial defenses (`UriPath.canonical` and `DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal`), but they are insufficient. `UriPath.canonical` only normalizes `/`-based traversal, while `StringUtils.cleanPath` later converts `\\\\` to `/` and resolves `..`, so a backslash-based payload can change meaning after validation. The final containment check is also flawed because it uses a plain prefix test (`startsWith`) rather than a segment-aware canonical/real-path check. This is therefore a real CWE-022 issue, not an A1/A2/B1/C1 false positive.",
  "FP_Reason": [],
  "evidence": "Relevant flow: `StaticFilesConfiguration.consumeWithFileResourceHandlers()` -> `staticResourceHandler.getResource(httpRequest)` -> `AbstractResourceHandler.getResource(HttpServletRequest)` -> `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource(String)` -> `new ExternalResource(addedPath)` -> later `resource.getInputStream()` is copied to the HTTP response. Key code: `ExternalResource(String path) { file = new File(StringUtils.cleanPath(path)); }`; `ExternalResourceHandler.getResource`: `path = UriPath.canonical(path); String addedPath = addPaths(baseResource, path); ExternalResource resource = new ExternalResource(addedPath); ... if (resource != null && resource.exists()) { DirectoryTraversal.protectAgainstForExternal(resource.getPath(), baseResource); return resource; }`; `DirectoryTraversal.isPathWithinFolder`: `return rlatsPath.startsWith(rlatsFolder);`. Concrete exploit: if an application enables `externalStaticFileLocation(\"/var/www\")` and a file exists at `/var/www2/secret.txt`, a request like `/..%5cwww2%5csecret.txt` can bypass the checks. `UriPath.canonical(\"/..\\\\www2\\\\secret.txt\")` does not neutralize the backslash traversal; `StringUtils.cleanPath(\"/var/www/..\\\\www2\\\\secret.txt\")` becomes `/var/www2/secret.txt`; the guard then checks `\"var/www2/secret.txt\".startsWith(\"var/www\")`, which is true, so the file is incorrectly treated as inside the static root and will be streamed by `IOUtils.copy(inputStream, wrappedOutputStream)`. The existing test `StaticFilesTestExternal.testDirectoryTraversalProtectionExternal()` does not rule this out because it exercises a different path and only shows one non-exploiting case returning 404.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
